draft.introduction: Analects 4:15 contains Confucius's statement that his Way is unified by a single thread. His disciple Zengzi identifies this thread as zhong (loyalty, doing one's best) and shu (reciprocity, empathy).

translation.text: The Master said: "Shen, my Way is bound together by one single thread." Master Zeng said: "Indeed." When the Master had left, the other disciples asked: "What did he mean?" Master Zeng said: "The Way of the Master consists of doing one's best (zhong) and treating others as one would wish to be treated (shu). That is all."
translation.original_text: 子曰：参乎！吾道一以贯之。曾子曰：唯。

translator_notes.note: The concept of shu (reciprocity) is often compared to the Golden Rule found in virtually every major ethical tradition. Confucius formulates it negatively in Analects 15:24: "Do not impose on others what you do not wish for yourself." The pairing with zhong (conscientiousness, doing one's utmost) adds an active dimension: it is not enough to refrain from harm; one must actively exert oneself on behalf of others.
